source.ru: Древнеисландский текст, ред. Eddukvæði. Sæmundar-Edda. Guðni Jónsson bjó til prentunar. Перевод: Т. Ермолаев.
source.en: An ancient Icelandic text, edited by Eddukvæði. Sæmundar-Edda. Guðni Jónsson bjó til prentunar. Translation by Lee M. Hollander (1936).

lines: I Maðr hét Gestumblindi, ríkr ok mikill óvinr Heiðreks konungs. Konungr sendi honum orð, at hann kæmi á fund hans at sættast við hann, ef hann vill halda lífinu. Gestumblindi var ekki spekingr mikill, ok fyrir þá sök, at hann veit sik vanfæran til at skipta orðum við konunginn, hann veit ok, at þungt mun vera at hlíta dómi spekinganna, því at sakir eru nógar, þat ráð tekur Gestumblindi, at hann blótar Óðin til fulltings sér ok biðr hann líta á sitt mál ok heitr honum miklum gæðum.

II Eitt kveld er þar drepit á dyrr síðla, ok gengr Gestumblindi  til hurðar ok sér mann kominn. Hann spyrr þann at nafni, en hann nefndist Gestumblindi ok mælti, at þeir skyldu klæðum skipta, ok svá gera þeir. Bóndi ferr nú í brott ok felr sik, en komandinn gengr inn, ok þykkjast allir þar kenna Gestumblinda, ok líðr af nóttin.
III Um daginn eptir gerir sjá Gestumblindi för sína á fund konungs, ok hann kvaddi vel konunginn. Konungr þagði.
IV «Herra,» segir hann, «því kom ek hingat, at ek vil við yðr sættast.»
V Þá svarar konungr: «Viltu þola dóm spekinga minna?»
VI Hann segir: «Er ekki fleiri undanlausnir?»
VII Konungr mælti: «Vera skulu fleiri, ef þú þykkist til færr at bera upp gátur.»
VIII Gestumblindi segir: «Lítt mun ek til þess færr, enda mun harðr á annat borð þykkja.»
IX «Viltu,» segir konungr, «heldr þola dóm spekinga minna?»
X «Þat kýs ek,» segir hann, «at bera fyrr upp gáturnar.»
XI «Þat er rétt ok vel fallit,» segir konungr.
þat er ek hafða í gær,

XII Konungr segir: «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumblindi, getit er þessar. Fæ­ri honum mungát. Þat lemr mar­gra vit, ok margir eru þá mar­g­mál­gari, er mungát ferr á, en su­mum vefst tungan, svá at ekki verðr at orði.»
XIII «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumb­lin­di, getit er þessar. Þar fórtu yfir árbrú, ok var árvegr undir þér, en fuglar flugu yfir höfði þér ok hjá þér tveim megin, ok var þat þeira vegr.»
XIV  «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumb­lindi, getit er þessar. Þar lagðist þú í forsælu, er dögg var fallin á grasi, ok kældir svá varir þínar ok stöðvaðir svá þorsta þinn.»
ok hefir hann þær fyrr of farit,
XV «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumblin­di, getit er þessar. Þat er hamarr sá, er hafðr er at gullsmíð; hann kveðr hátt við, er hann kemr á harðan steðja, ok þat er hans gata.»
XVI «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumblindi, getit er þessar. Þat eru smiðbelgir; þeir hafa engan vind, nema þeim sé blásit ok eru þeir dauðir sem annat smíði, en fyrir þeim má líkt smíða sverð sem annat.»
ok berr ofar kné en kvið?
getit er þessar. Þat er laukr. Höfuð
hans er fast í jörðu, en hann kvíslar,
XIX Heiðrekr mælti: «Smækkast nú gáturnar, Gestumblindi, hvat þarf lengr yfir þessu at sitja? Þat er hrafntinna, ok skein á hana sólar­ geisli.»

XX «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumb­lindi, getit er þessar. Þar fara svanbrúðir til hreiðrs síns ok verpa eggjum; skurm á eggi er eigi höndum gert né hamri klappat, en svanr er fyrir eyjar utan örðigr, sá er þær gátu eggin við.»
þar til er mög of getr,
XXI «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumb­lin­di, getit er þessar. Þat eru hvannir tvær ok hvannarkálfr á milli þei­ra.»
XXII «Góð er gáta þín, Gestum­blin­di, getit er þessar. Þar fanntu hest dauðan á ísjaka ok orm dauðan á hestinum, ok rak þat allt saman eptir ánni.»
XXIII «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumb­lin­di, getit er þessar. Þat er Ítrekr ok Andaðr, er þeir sitja at tafli sínu.»
XXIV «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumb­lin­di, getit er þessar. Þat er hnettafl; inar dekkri verja hnefann, en hvít­ar sækja.»
þar mun hann sinn aldr ala?
XXV «Þat er eldr fólginn á arni, ok tekr ór tinnu.»
ok yrkir á sól til saka?
XXVI «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumb­lindi, getit er þessar. Þat er myrkvi; hann líðr yfir jörðina, svá at ekki sér fyrir honum ok eigi sól, en hann er af, þegar vind gerir á.»
XXVIII «Þat er skjöldr; hann verðr opt blóðugr í bardögum ok hlífir vel þeim mönnum, er skjaldfimir eru.»
XXIX «Þat eru rjúpur; þær eru hvítar um vetr, en svartar um sumar.»
við þat munu þær aldr ala?
XXX «Þat eru Hlés brúðir, er svá heita.»
XXXI «Þat eru bylgjur, er svá heita.»
XXXII «Þat eru Ægis ekkjur, svá hei­ta öldur.»
XXXIII «Þar hafði önd búit hreiðr sitt í milli nautskjálka, ok lá haussinn ofan yfir.»
ef hann hefir sér vel traustan vin?
XXXIV «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumblindi, getit er þessar. Þat er akkeri með góðum streng; ef fleinn hans er í grunni, þá bergr þat.»
XXXV «Þat eru bárur, en beðir þeira eru sker ok urðir, en þær verða lítt sénar í logni.»
XXXVI «Þar drukku grísir gylti, en hún hrein við.»
XXXVII Konungr mælti þá: «Ef þú ert sá Gestumblindi, sem ek hugða, þá ertu vitrari en ek ætlaða. En frá gyltinni segir þú nú úti í garðinum.»
XXXVIII Þá lét konungr drepa gyltina, ok hafði hún níu grísi, sem Gestumblindi sagði. Nú grunar konung, hverr mað­ rinn mun vera.
XXXIX «Góð er gáta þín, Gestumb­lindi, getit er þessar. Þat er kýr.»
XL «Þar saztu á vegg ok sátt val bera æði í hamra.»
XLI «Þat er þá, er Óðinn ríðr Sle­ipni.»
XLII Heiðrekr konungr segir: «Þat veiztu einn, rög vættr.»
XLIII Ok þá bregðr Heiðrekr Tyrfingi ok höggr til hans, en Óðinn brást þá í valslíki ok fló á brott. En ko­nungr hjó eptir ok af honum vé­lifiðrit aptan, ok því er valr svá vé­listuttr ávallt síðan.
XLIV Óðinn mælti þá: «Fyrir þat, Hei­ðrekr konungr, er þú rétt til mín ok vildir drepa mik saklausan, skulu þér inir verstu þrælar at bana ver­ða.»
XLV Eptir þat skilr með þeim.

I Одного могущественного человека звали Гестумблинди, он был большой недруг конунга Хейдрека. Конунг послал ему сообщение, чтобы тот отправился к нему для примирения, если хочет сохранить жизнь. Гестумблинди не был великим мудрецом, считал себя неспособным обменяться речами с конунгом и знал, что тяжело полагаться на суд мудрецов, ибо обвинений будет достаточно. Тогда Гестумблинди решил, что он принесёт жертвы Одину в помощь себе, попросит его рассмотреть своё дело и пообещает ему большие дары.

II Поздно вечером в дверь постучали, Гестумблинди подошёл и увидел, что явился некий человек. Он спросил у него имя, а тот назвался Гестумблинди и сказал, что они должны поменяться одеждой, так они и сделали. Затем бонд ушёл прочь и спрятался, а пришелец вошёл, и все приняли его за Гестумблинди, и так прошла ночь.
III На следующий день этот Гестумблинди отправился на встречу с конунгом. Он хорошо поприветствовал его. Конунг промолчал.
IV «Государь, — сказал он, — потому пришёл я сюда, что хочу с Вами помириться».
V Тогда конунг спросил: «Хочешь подвергнуться суду моих мудрецов?»
VI Он сказал: «Нет ли других способов выкупа?»
VII Конунг ответил: «Будет их больше, если ты можешь предложить загадки».
VIII Гестумблинди сказал: «Мало я к этому способен, даже если другой выбор кажется трудным».
IX «Ты всё-таки хочешь, — спросил конунг, — подвергнуться суду моих мудрецов?»
X «Лучше я выберу, — сказал он, — загадывать загадки».
XI «Это правильно и вполне подойдёт, — сказал конунг».

XII Конунг говорит: «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Принесите ему пиво. У многих оно сокрушает ум, и многие становятся слишком болтливыми, когда подносят пиво, а у некоторых заплетаются языки, так что они не могут вымолвить ни слова».

XIII «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Ты шёл по мосту через реку, и под тобой была речная дорога, а птицы летели над твоей головой и рядом с тобой с двух сторон, и это была их дорога.»
блинди, отгадана она. Ты лежал в
тени, когда на траву выпала роса,
и так остудил свои губы и утолил
XV «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Это молот, который используют для ковки золота; он громко вскрикивает, когда попадает по твёрдой наковальне, и это его дорога».
XVI «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Это кузнечные меха; в них нет никакого ветра, если их не надуют, и они мертвы, как любая вещь, но с ними можно выковать меч, как и ещё что-то».
XVIII «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Это лук. Его голова крепко сидит в земле, и он ветвится, когда растёт».
XIX Хейдрек сказал: «Теперь загадки становятся проще, Гестумблинди, зачем дальше над ними сидеть? Это обсидиан, и на него посветил солнечный луч».

XX «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Это самки лебедя идут в своё гнездо и несут яйца; яичная скорлупа не руками сделана, не молотком сколочена, а высящийся вне острова — это лебедь, от которого они отложили яйца».
и нет у этих жён мужей?
XXI «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Это два дягиля и побег дягиля между ними».
XXII «Хороша твоя загадка, Гесту­м­ блинди, отгадана она. Ты нашёл мёртвого коня на льдине и мёртвую змею на этом коне, и всех вместе их несло по реке».
XXIII «Хороша твоя загадка, Гесту­м­блинди, отгадана она. Это Ит­рек и Андад, которые сидят и играют в тавлеи».
XXIV «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Это хнеттавль 4; тёмные защищают короля, а белые нападают».
XXV «Это огонь, скрытый в очаге, он загорается от кремня».
XXVI «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Это туман; он парит над землёй, и из-за него ничего не видно, и нет солнца, но он уходит, когда подует ветер».
XXVIII «Это щит; он часто становит­ ся окровавленным в битвах и хорошо бережёт людей, которые опытны со щитом».
XXIX «Это куропатки; они белые зимой, и чёрные летом».
XXX «Это невесты Хлера, как их называют».
и нет у этих жён мужей?
XXXII «Это вдовы Эгира, так называются волны».
XXXIII «Это утка построила своё гнездо между челюстей быка, и сверху лежал череп».
XXXIV «Хороша твоя загадка, Гесту­мблинди, отгадана она. Это якорь с добрым канатом; если его лапа во дне, это помогает».
XXXV «Это волны, их ложе — скалы и камни, а в тихую погоду их становится мало».
XXXVI «Там поросята сосали свинью, а она визжала от этого».
XXXVII Тогда конунг сказал: «Если ты — тот Гестумблинди, о котором я думал, то ты мудрее, чем я предполагал. А сейчас ты говоришь о свинье снаружи на дворе».
XXXVIII Конунг велел убить свинью, и в ней было девять поросят, как и сказал Гестумблинди. Тут конунг заподозрил, кто этот человек.
XXXIX «Хороша твоя загадка, Гестумблинди, отгадана она. Это корова».
XL «Ты сидел там на стене и видел сокола, несущего в скалы гагу».
XLI «Это Один скачет на Слейпнире». Тогда Гестумблинди сказал:
XLII Конунг Хейдрек говорит: «Об этом знаешь только ты, мерзкая тварь!»

XLIII И тогда Хейдрек замахнулся Тюрвингом и рубанул по нему. Но Один обернулся соколом и полетел прочь. А конунг ударил его вслед и отсёк сзади хвостовое оперение, и поэтому сокол с тех пор такой короткохвостый.
XLIV Тогда Один сказал: «За то, что ты, конунг Хейдрек, посягнул на меня и хотел убить невиновного, тебя погубят наихудшие рабы».
"That would I have which I had yesterday;
Aright read now this riddle, Heithrek!"
give him ale! That hampers many a man’s wits; by it,
some become talkative, but other men’s wits are mazed."
"From home I hied me, and from home faring
I saw a way of ways:
was a way beneath and a way above,
and ways there were on all sides.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
You went over a bridge, the river ran under it, the birds flew above your head and on either side of you—that was their way."
"What drink was it I drank yesterday?
neither mead nor ale, nor meat, either;
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
you lay down in the shade where the dew had fallen on the grass, and this cooled your lips and quenched your thirst."
"Harshly he clangs, on hard paths treading
Two mouths he has, and mightily kisses,
and on gold alone he goes.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is the goldsmith’s hammer with which gold is beaten."
"Who is the great one that glides o’er the earth,
and swallows both waters and woods?
The wind he fears, but wights nowise,
and seeks to harm the sun.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
That is the fog. One cannot see the sun because of him, but he disappears when the wind blows, and men can do naught against him. He kills the light of the sun."
"Who is the mighty one who o’er much has sway,
the half of whom turns toward Hel?
He saves many men but slashes the earth,
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
That is the anchor, with a stout and strong rope. It guards many a ship. It grips the earth with one fluke which thus turns Hel-ward. Many a man has been saved by it."
"On high fells what lives? What falls in deep dales?
Without air what lives? What is not ever silent?
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
the raven always lives on high fells, the dew always falls in deep dales, the fish live without air, and the roaring waterfall is never silent."
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
with his head ever Hel-ward turning,
with his feet seeking the sun.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
That is the leek: its head turns into the earth, but the leaves, upward."
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
Were twain briskly, and breathless withal.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
they are the smith’s bellows; they blow but have no breath."
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
The white fliers on the flagstones bounded.
but the swart ones sank in the sand.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
they are hail and rain; because the hail beats on the flags, but the rain drops sink in the sand and go into the ground."
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
A black boar saw I bask in the mud,
yet no bristles stood on his back.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
Ten tongues has it, has twenty eyes
and forty feet, slowly fares that wight.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
you saw a sow in the farmyard with nine shoats in her. Then the king had the sow killed, and nine pigs were found in her, just as Gestumblindi had said."
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
Upward it flies with eagle’s voice,
and hard grip its claws the helmet.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
Eight feet it has and four eyes,
and its knees are above its belly.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
It lights for men, and swallows up lights,
and wolves seek ever to win it.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is the Sun. He lights all the world and shines on all men; but there are two wolves, hight Skalli and Hatti, one of whom goes before, and the other follows, the sun."
"What marvel is it which without I saw,
Harder than horn, blacker than Hel,
whiter than shell of egg, straighter than shaft of spear.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is obsidian, with the sun shining on it."
"Two brides did bear, white-blond their locks,
and house-maids were they ale-casks homeward;
were they not shaped by hand nor by hammers wrought;
yet upright sate he on the isles, who made them.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
you saw two female swans going to their nests to lay their eggs. Their shells are neither hand-made nor wrought by hammers; but the he-swan sits out on the islands, he that gat their eggs with them."
"Who be the women on wilding fell?
One bears a babe by the other,
and maid by maid a man-child begets,
yet man has not touched these maids.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
they are two angelica stalks standing together, with a shoot coming up between them."
for their king kill each other?
Every day the dark ones shield him,
but the fair ones aye go forth.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
That is the game of hneftafl: weaponless, the figures slay each other for the sake of their king. The red ones are his followers."
"Who be the playmates that pass over the lands,
A white shield they show in winter,
and a swart one in summer.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
they are the ptarmigans. They are white in winter and black in summer."
"Who be the sisters that sorrowing fare,
Many a man their might hath known
and thus they live their lives.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
They are the billows. They are called the maids of Ægir."
"Who be the maids that fare, many together,
Whitish hair have the white-hooded ones,
and no man is with the maids.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
but these are the waves, as before."
"Who be the widows that band all together,
They are seldom kind to sailor-folk,
and are wide awake in the wind.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
they are Ægir’s widows. Thus are called the breakers."
"Who be the women that wade in the surf
A hard bed have the white-hooded ones,
and quiet are they in calm.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
they are the billows—their beds are skerries and shingle, and they become somewhat sluggish in calm weather."
a corpse sate on a corpse;
one blind rode a blind thing to the billows-road,
and no life was in the nag.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
you came to a river, and an ice-floe floated down it. On the floe lay a dead horse, and on the horse, a dead snake; so that one blind thing rode another blind thing."
"What creature kills the cattle men have,
Eight horns it has, but head it has none,
and runs when run it may.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is the húnn on the checkerboard. It shares its name with the bear, and it runs as soon as it is overthrown."
"What beast is it which brave men shelters?
It has a bloody back and wards off blows,
goes against spears, life-giving to some.
Against lords left hand it lays its body.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is the shield. It often becomes bloody in battle, shielding those men who are handy with it."
"Greatly had grown a goose with big neb,
had brought timber together, for goslings eager.
Gave shelter to her the sheaves bite-swords;
but above it lay the drink’s dinful-rockcave,
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is a duck which had built her nest between the jaws of an ox, with the skull roofing above it."
"Four do hang and four do gang;
two show the way, two ward off dogs;
one drags after, most often dirty.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is a cow. She has four feet and four tits, two horns and two eyes, and her tail drags after her."
"Who is the one that in the ashpit sleeps:
and is only struck out of stone?
Neither father nor mother has the greedy fiend
there he wants to live his life.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is the fire hidden in the hearth—it is struck out of flint."
he put buttock neath belly and bobbed with his tail.
Draw it out and in, work at it long!
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that horse is a piece of linen on the loom. His mare is the weaver’s reed, and up and down is the web to be shaken."
"Who be the thanes to the thing who ride?
Their liege-men send they over all the lands,
to seek a place to settle.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
they are Itrek and Andath sitting on their checkerboard."
"In the summer saw I, at sunset time,
a merry band at festive board;
the men did drink their mead in peace,
the while the mead-keg muttering stood.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is a sow with her shoats. When the pigs suck her she grunts, while they keep quiet."
"Maidens saw I which were much like dust;
Are they swart and sallow in sunny weather,
but the fairer, the fainter the light.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
they are the gledes dying on the hearth."
"On a sail I sate and saw dead men
who a blood-vein bore to the bark of a tree.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
You sate on a wall and saw a falcon bear an eider duck to the cliff."
"Who are the twain that on ten feet run?
three eyes they have, but only one tail.
Aright guess now this riddle, Heithrek!"
that is Óthin riding on Sleipnir."
"Guess one more riddle, since wiser thou seemest
what said Óthin in Baldr’s ear
before he was borne to the fire?"
the words thou spakest no wight knoweth
but thou, ill and unclean wight."
